trivia: Brazil is the most successful nation in World Cup history with 5 titles (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002).
Brazil is the only country to have played in every single World Cup since the tournament began in 1930.
The first World Cup was held in Uruguay in 1930, and the hosts won it.
Miroslav Klose of Germany is the all-time top scorer at World Cups with 16 goals.
Just Fontaine scored 13 goals in a single World Cup (1958) — still the record for one tournament.
Pelé is the only player to win three World Cups (1958, 1962 and 1970).
Germany and Brazil have both reached the most World Cup finals.
The fastest goal in World Cup history was scored by Hakan Şükür after just 11 seconds (2002).
Norman Whiteside is the youngest player to appear at a World Cup, aged 17 (1982).
The 2014 final between Germany and Argentina was decided by a Mario Götze goal in extra time.
Italy and Brazil were the first nations to win the World Cup on foreign soil.
The highest-scoring World Cup match was Austria 7–5 Switzerland in 1954 (12 goals).
The World Cup trophy used since 1974 is made of 18-carat gold and stands 36.8 cm tall.
Only eight different nations have ever won the World Cup.
France won their first World Cup as hosts in 1998, beating Brazil 3–0.
The 1950 final phase is the only World Cup decided by a final group stage, not a single final.
Cameroon's Roger Milla, aged 42, is the oldest goalscorer in World Cup history (1994).
Argentina won the 2022 World Cup in Qatar, with Lionel Messi finally lifting the trophy.
Kylian Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the 2022 final — and still finished on the losing side.
The 1970 Brazil side is widely regarded as the greatest World Cup team ever assembled.
Penalty shootouts were first used to decide a World Cup match in 1982.
Diego Maradona's 'Hand of God' and 'Goal of the Century' both came in the same 1986 quarter-final.
The 2026 World Cup will be the first hosted by three nations: USA, Canada and Mexico.
The 2026 tournament will expand to 48 teams, the largest World Cup ever.
England's only World Cup title came on home soil in 1966.
Spain won their first and only World Cup in 2010, scoring just 8 goals all tournament.
Uruguay, despite their size, has won the World Cup twice (1930 and 1950).
Lothar Matthäus holds the record for most World Cup matches played, with 25 games.
The Maracanã hosted a record crowd of nearly 200,000 for the 1950 final.
Goal-line technology was used at a World Cup for the first time in 2014.
The original World Cup trophy was named the Jules Rimet Cup after FIFA's third president.
Brazil were allowed to keep the Jules Rimet trophy permanently after their third win in 1970.
The Jules Rimet trophy was stolen in England in 1966 and found by a dog named Pickles.
The Jules Rimet trophy was stolen again in Brazil in 1983 and has never been recovered.
Mexico was the first country to host the World Cup twice, in 1970 and 1986.
The 1942 and 1946 World Cups were cancelled because of the Second World War.
Italy and Brazil are the only nations to have won the World Cup in back-to-back tournaments.
The 1930 World Cup featured just 13 teams, all invited by hosts Uruguay.
The USA reached the semi-finals of the very first World Cup in 1930.
The first goal in World Cup history was scored by France's Lucien Laurent in 1930.
West Germany's 1954 triumph over Hungary is remembered as the 'Miracle of Bern'.
Hungary's Golden Team went years unbeaten but lost the 1954 final they were favoured to win.
The 1958 World Cup in Sweden saw a 17-year-old Pelé burst onto the world stage.
Pelé is the youngest player to score in a World Cup final, doing so at 17 in 1958.
England's 1966 final win over West Germany featured a disputed Geoff Hurst goal off the crossbar.
Geoff Hurst is the only player to score a hat-trick in a men's World Cup final, in 1966.
The 1970 final in Mexico was the first World Cup final broadcast in colour.
Substitutes and red and yellow cards were both introduced at the 1970 World Cup.
Gerd Müller scored 14 World Cup goals in just two tournaments, 1970 and 1974.
The Netherlands' 'Total Football' dazzled in the 1970s but lost the 1974 and 1978 finals.
Johan Cruyff led the Netherlands to the 1974 final but never won a World Cup.
Argentina won their first World Cup as hosts in 1978.
The current World Cup trophy was designed by Italian sculptor Silvio Gazzaniga.
The 1982 World Cup in Spain expanded the tournament to 24 teams.
Italy's Paolo Rossi won the Golden Boot and led his country to the title in 1982.
The 1982 semi-final between France and West Germany was the first decided on penalties.
The 1990 World Cup in Italy had the lowest goals-per-game average in the tournament's history.
Cameroon stunned defending champions Argentina in the opening match of Italia 90.
The 1994 World Cup in the USA remains the best-attended tournament in history.
The 1994 final between Brazil and Italy was the first decided by a penalty shootout.
Roberto Baggio skied the decisive penalty as Italy lost the 1994 final to Brazil.
The Golden Goal rule was used at the World Cup from 1998 until it was abolished after 2002.
Laurent Blanc scored the first Golden Goal in World Cup history for France in 1998.
Zinedine Zidane scored twice with his head in the 1998 final for France.
The 2002 World Cup was the first hosted by two countries, South Korea and Japan.
The 2002 World Cup was the first held in Asia.
South Korea reached the semi-finals as co-hosts in 2002, their best-ever finish.
Ronaldo scored both goals as Brazil beat Germany in the 2002 final.
Ronaldo finished the 2002 World Cup as top scorer with eight goals.
Zinedine Zidane was sent off for headbutting Marco Materazzi in the 2006 final.
Italy won the 2006 World Cup on penalties against France.
The 2010 World Cup in South Africa was the first held on the African continent.
The vuvuzela became the buzzing soundtrack of the 2010 World Cup.
Spain became the first team to win a World Cup after losing their opening match, in 2010.
Andrés Iniesta scored the only goal of the 2010 final, in extra time.
Germany thrashed hosts Brazil 7–1 in the 2014 World Cup semi-final.
Brazil's 7–1 loss to Germany in 2014 was their heaviest defeat in World Cup history.
Klose passed Ronaldo's record to become the all-time top scorer in that same 2014 match.
James Rodríguez won the 2014 Golden Boot with six goals for Colombia.
The 2018 World Cup in Russia was the first held in Eastern Europe.
Croatia reached their first-ever World Cup final in 2018, losing to France.
The Video Assistant Referee was used at a World Cup for the first time in 2018.
The 2022 World Cup in Qatar was the first held in the Arab world.
Qatar 2022 was the first World Cup played in November and December rather than the summer.
Saudi Arabia stunned eventual champions Argentina in the group stage of the 2022 World Cup.
Morocco became the first African and first Arab nation to reach a World Cup semi-final, in 2022.
Lionel Messi won his second Golden Ball as the best player of the 2022 World Cup.
The 2022 final between Argentina and France finished 3–3 before being settled on penalties.
Mário Zagallo and Franz Beckenbauer have each won the World Cup as both player and manager.
Didier Deschamps won the World Cup as France's captain in 1998 and as their manager in 2018.
Vittorio Pozzo is the only manager to win the World Cup twice, with Italy in 1934 and 1938.
Antonio Carbajal of Mexico was the first player to appear at five World Cups.
Lionel Messi has played more World Cup matches than any other man.
Iceland is the smallest nation by population ever to qualify for a men's World Cup, debuting in 2018.
Trinidad and Tobago were the smallest country by population to qualify, back in 2006.
Brazil's Garrincha and Pelé never lost a match they played together for the national team.
The World Cup is the most widely viewed sporting event on the planet.
FIFA awards a Golden Boot to the top scorer and a Golden Ball to the best player of each tournament.
The Golden Glove award honours the best goalkeeper of each World Cup.
A brand-new official match ball is produced for every World Cup.
The 2026 World Cup will feature 104 matches, the most in the tournament's history.
Mexico will become the first country to host or co-host the men's World Cup three times in 2026.
Eusébio top-scored at the 1966 World Cup with nine goals, carrying Portugal to third place.
The 1938 World Cup was the last before a 12-year gap caused by the Second World War.
Argentina's Mario Kempes was the standout star and top scorer of the 1978 World Cup.
Salvatore Schillaci's six goals lit up Italia 90 and won him the Golden Boot.
Davor Šuker won the 1998 Golden Boot as Croatia finished third on their World Cup debut.
Oleg Salenko scored five goals in a single 1994 match for Russia against Cameroon, a record.
Toni Schumacher's brutal challenge in the 1982 semi-final remains one of the World Cup's most infamous fouls.
Marco Tardelli's celebration after scoring in the 1982 final is one of football's iconic images.
